enrichment.short_description: Redis is an open-source, in-memory data structure store used as a database, cache, message broker, and streaming engine, known for sub-millisecond performance.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium and usage-based: Redis Open Source is free to self-host; managed Redis Cloud offers free, pay-as-you-go, and enterprise tiers
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Redis Open Source and Redis Cloud free tier); Redis Cloud paid plans start around $5/month (Essentials) with Pro from about $200/month
languages: Core written in C; official and community client libraries for Python, Java, Node.js, Go, C#, Ruby, PHP, and more
enrichment.seo_title: Redis Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Licensing Explained
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See what Redis costs in 2026, how its AGPLv3 licensing works after the 2024-2025 changes, and whether the in-memory database fits your stack.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Redis is a free, open-source, in-memory data structure store originally created in 2009 by Salvatore Sanfilippo (known online as antirez), who used it to solve real-time analytics problems at his startup. Redis is commonly used as a database, cache, message broker, and streaming engine, prized for sub-millisecond read and write latency and native support for data structures such as strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams, and geospatial indexes. The company behind Redis, originally called Redis Labs and now known as Redis Ltd. (also referred to as Redis Inc.), was founded in 2011 by Ofer Bengal and Yiftach Shoolman and is headquartered in San Francisco, California, after relocating from Mountain View in 2024; as of 2025-2026 the company employs roughly 1,500 people and reported around 272.6 million dollars in annual recurring revenue with a valuation near 2 billion dollars. Redis's licensing has changed significantly in recent years, which is important for anyone evaluating the project: the software was originally released under the permissive BSD 3-Clause license, but in March 2024 Redis Labs switched future versions to a dual license of the Redis Source Available License (RSALv2) and the Server Side Public License (SSPLv1), moves that were not recognized as open source by the Open Source Initiative and prompted the community-led Valkey fork under the Linux Foundation. After that licensing change failed to gain community acceptance, and following antirez's return to the company as a developer advocate in mid-2024, Redis reversed course in May 2025 and began offering Redis 8 and later versions under a tri-license model that includes the AGPLv3 open-source license alongside RSALv2 and SSPLv1, effectively bringing Redis back into OSI-recognized open source territory. As part of this update, previously separate Redis Stack modules, RediSearch, RedisJSON, RedisTimeSeries, and RedisBloom, were folded into Redis 8 as built-in capabilities. For teams that do not want to self-host, Redis Ltd. sells a managed Redis Cloud service with a free tier (30 MB, shared infrastructure), Essentials plans priced roughly 5 to 216 dollars per month for dedicated small-to-mid-size databases, and Pro plans starting around 200 dollars per month for production workloads on dedicated VPC infrastructure with multi-availability-zone high availability and a 99.999 percent uptime SLA; Enterprise and self-managed Redis Software options are also available with custom pricing. Because of its speed and flexible data structures, Redis is widely used for caching, session storage, real-time leaderboards, rate limiting, pub/sub messaging, and increasingly as a vector database for AI and retrieval-augmented generation workloads.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Redis began as a personal project by Salvatore Sanfilippo in 2009 and has grown into one of the most widely deployed in-memory data stores in the world, used as a cache, primary database, message broker, and streaming engine by companies of all sizes.

The company behind Redis, Redis Ltd. (formerly Redis Labs), is headquartered in San Francisco and has roughly 1,500 employees. Its licensing history is notable: a March 2024 shift away from BSD to source-available licenses (RSALv2/SSPLv1) triggered the Valkey community fork, before Redis reversed course in May 2025 and adopted a tri-license including AGPLv3 for Redis 8 and later.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Redis supports rich native data structures beyond simple key-value pairs, including hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, streams, and geospatial indexes, all operating in memory for sub-millisecond latency.

Recent versions bundle capabilities that were previously separate Redis Stack modules, such as full-text search (RediSearch), native JSON support (RedisJSON), time-series data (RedisTimeSeries), and probabilistic data structures (RedisBloom), plus vector search for AI and RAG applications.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Redis Open Source is free to download and self-host under the current tri-license (AGPLv3, RSALv2, SSPLv1). For teams that prefer a managed service, Redis Cloud offers a free tier (30 MB), Essentials plans from about 5 to 216 dollars per month, and Pro plans starting around 200 dollars per month with multi-AZ high availability and a 99.999 percent SLA.
Enterprise and self-managed Redis Software deployments are available with custom pricing for organizations with specific compliance, on-premises, or private-cloud requirements.

features.title: In-memory data structures
features.description: Native support for strings, hashes, lists, sets, sorted sets, and streams enables far more than simple key-value caching.

features.title: Persistence options
features.description: RDB snapshotting and append-only file (AOF) logging let Redis balance performance with durability.

features.title: Pub/Sub and streams
features.description: Built-in publish/subscribe messaging and Redis Streams support real-time event and messaging architectures.

features.title: Bundled search and JSON
features.description: RediSearch and RedisJSON capabilities are now built into Redis 8, enabling full-text search and native JSON document storage.

features.title: Replication and clustering
features.description: Redis Cluster and replication provide horizontal scaling and high availability for production workloads.

features.title: Vector search for AI/RAG
features.description: Native vector data types and similarity search support retrieval-augmented generation and AI application backends.

features.title: Lua scripting and functions
features.description: Server-side scripting allows atomic, custom logic to run directly inside Redis.

features.title: Broad client ecosystem
features.description: Official and community-maintained client libraries exist for nearly every major programming language.

pros: Extremely fast in-memory performance for caching and real-time workloads
Rich set of native data structures beyond simple key-value pairs
Now available under AGPLv3, restoring OSI-recognized open-source licensing as of Redis 8
Large ecosystem of client libraries and community tooling
Managed Redis Cloud reduces operational overhead for teams that do not want to self-host
Built-in vector search suits modern AI and RAG use cases
cons: The 2024 licensing change to SSPL/RSALv2 damaged community trust, even after the 2025 AGPL reversal
In-memory model means data sets are constrained by available RAM, raising costs at scale
Some advanced enterprise features still require the commercial Redis Cloud or Redis Software tiers
The Valkey fork (Linux Foundation) has pulled some contributors and users away from Redis
Complex multi-node cluster configurations require real operational expertise
Data durability requires careful persistence tuning between RDB and AOF trade-offs

faqs.question: Is Redis free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. Redis Open Source is free to self-host under its current tri-license, which includes the AGPLv3 open-source license alongside RSALv2 and SSPLv1.

faqs.question: What license does Redis use in 2026?
faqs.answer: Redis 8 and later versions ship under a tri-license: AGPLv3, RSALv2, and SSPLv1, adopted in May 2025 after the March 2024 move away from BSD licensing.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Redis and Valkey?
faqs.answer: Valkey is a community fork of Redis created by the Linux Foundation in response to Redis's March 2024 licensing change; it remains under the original BSD-style permissive license.

faqs.question: Who created Redis?
faqs.answer: Salvatore Sanfilippo (antirez) created Redis in 2009; the commercial company behind it, now Redis Ltd., was founded in 2011.

faqs.question: What does Redis Cloud cost?
faqs.answer: Redis Cloud has a free tier (30 MB), Essentials plans from about 5 to 216 dollars per month, and Pro plans starting around 200 dollars per month.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Redis for free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Redis Open Source can be self-hosted at no cost under its current AGPLv3-inclusive license.

faqs.question: What is Redis commonly used for?
faqs.answer: Caching, session storage, real-time analytics, message brokering, rate limiting, and increasingly vector search for AI applications.

faqs.question: Who owns Redis?
faqs.answer: Redis Ltd. (formerly Redis Labs), headquartered in San Francisco, California, owns and develops Redis.

use_cases.title: Application caching layer
use_cases.description: Reduce database load and speed up response times by caching frequently accessed data in memory.

use_cases.title: Real-time analytics and leaderboards
use_cases.description: Use sorted sets and streams to power real-time rankings, counters, and event pipelines.
use_cases.audience: Gaming, adtech, and real-time application teams

use_cases.title: AI and RAG vector backend
use_cases.description: Store and query vector embeddings alongside operational data for retrieval-augmented generation applications.

features: Built-in search, JSON, and vector capabilities
Up to 12 GB capacity at higher tiers

enrichment.short_description: Redmine is a free, open-source, web-based project management and issue-tracking tool built on Ruby on Rails.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free and open source (self-hosted); paid hosting and support available from third-party providers
languages: Built with Ruby on Rails; user interface available in dozens of languages (49+)
enrichment.seo_title: Redmine Review 2026: Free Open-Source Project Management Explained
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Everything you need to know about Redmine, the free, self-hosted, Ruby on Rails project management and issue tracker, including features and setup.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Redmine is a free, open-source, web-based project management and issue-tracking application originally created by Jean-Philippe Lang and first released in 2006. Built on the Ruby on Rails framework, Redmine is cross-platform and cross-database, running on operating systems such as Linux, Windows, and macOS, and supporting databases including MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQLite. It is distributed under the GNU General Public License version 2 (GPLv2), meaning the software itself is free to download, self-host, and modify with no licensing fees; organizations that want managed hosting, plugins, themes, or support typically pay third-party providers rather than a central Redmine vendor, since Redmine is a community-driven open-source project rather than a commercial company. As of recent releases (the 6.x series, including version 6.0.5), the project has accumulated close to 19,000 commits on its GitHub mirror and remains actively maintained by its open-source community. Redmine's core feature set covers multiple and simultaneous project and subproject management, flexible role-based access control, per-project wikis and forums, time tracking, Gantt charts and calendars for visual scheduling, a customizable issue tracker with configurable fields and workflows, and integration with popular version control systems including Git, Subversion, Mercurial, CVS, and Bazaar, complete with a built-in repository browser and diff viewer. It also supports email notifications, RSS and Atom feeds, LDAP authentication, self-registration, and a plugin architecture that has produced a large ecosystem of community-built extensions covering agile boards, additional reporting, and custom workflow automation. Because it is self-hosted and free, Redmine has been widely adopted by software development teams, educational institutions, government agencies, and non-profits that need a flexible, no-license-cost alternative to commercial project management tools like Jira. Its main trade-off compared to modern SaaS project management tools is that it requires organizations to install, maintain, and secure their own server infrastructure, or pay a third-party host, and its interface and general user experience are generally considered more utilitarian and dated than newer cloud-native competitors, though its deep customizability and zero licensing cost remain major draws for budget-conscious and infrastructure-savvy teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Redmine has been developed since 2006 by creator Jean-Philippe Lang and an open-source community, growing into one of the most widely used self-hosted project management and issue-tracking platforms, especially among software teams comfortable running their own infrastructure.

As a GPLv2-licensed project rather than a company product, Redmine has no official pricing, sales team, or vendor lock-in; its ongoing development, plugin ecosystem, and documentation are maintained collaboratively by contributors and third-party hosting providers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Redmine supports multiple simultaneous projects and subprojects, role-based access control, Gantt charts, calendars, and time tracking, giving teams the scheduling and reporting tools common to larger commercial PM tools.

It also integrates directly with version control systems like Git and Subversion, includes per-project wikis and forums, and offers a highly configurable issue tracker with customizable fields, statuses, and workflows, extendable further through a large community plugin ecosystem.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Redmine itself is entirely free under the GPLv2 open-source license, with no tiers, seats, or subscription fees for the core software.

Costs, if any, come from self-hosting infrastructure (servers, storage, maintenance) or from optional third-party managed hosting, support, or premium plugin providers, none of which are operated by a central Redmine company.

features.title: Multi-project and subproject management
features.description: Organize related work across parent and child projects with independent configuration.

features.title: Role-based access control
features.description: Granular permissions let administrators control what each role can view or edit per project.

features.title: Gantt charts and calendars
features.description: Visual scheduling tools help teams track deadlines and dependencies.

features.title: Time tracking
features.description: Built-in time logging supports effort tracking and reporting against issues and projects.

features.title: Per-project wikis and forums
features.description: Native documentation and discussion spaces live alongside each project's issues.

features.title: Version control integration
features.description: Built-in repository browser and diff viewer connect to Git, Subversion, Mercurial, CVS, and Bazaar.

features.title: Customizable issue tracker
features.description: Configurable fields, statuses, and workflows adapt Redmine to varied team processes.

features.title: Plugin ecosystem
features.description: A large library of community plugins extends Redmine with agile boards, reporting, and automation.

pros: Completely free and open source with no per-user licensing fees
Highly customizable issue trackers, workflows, and fields
Built-in Gantt charts, calendars, and time tracking
Native integration with major version control systems
Large plugin ecosystem for extending functionality
Self-hosting gives organizations full control over their data
cons: Requires self-hosting and server maintenance, or a paid third-party host
Interface feels dated compared to modern SaaS project management tools
No official vendor support; relies on community and third parties
Steeper setup and configuration learning curve than turnkey SaaS tools
Fewer native integrations with modern SaaS apps than commercial competitors
Mobile experience is limited compared to native mobile-first tools

faqs.question: Is Redmine free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Redmine is free and open source under the GNU General Public License v2, with no licensing fees for the core software.

faqs.question: Who created Redmine?
faqs.answer: Redmine was created by Jean-Philippe Lang and first released in 2006.

faqs.question: What is Redmine built with?
faqs.answer: Redmine is built on the Ruby on Rails framework and supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQLite databases.

faqs.question: Can Redmine integrate with Git?
faqs.answer: Yes, Redmine includes native integration with Git, Subversion, Mercurial, CVS, and Bazaar, plus a built-in repository browser.

faqs.question: Does Redmine offer official cloud hosting?
faqs.answer: Redmine itself does not offer official managed hosting; several third-party providers offer paid Redmine hosting and support.

faqs.question: What databases does Redmine support?
faqs.answer: Redmine supports MySQL, PostgreSQL, and SQLite, among other databases, due to its cross-database Rails architecture.

faqs.question: Is Redmine similar to Jira?
faqs.answer: Yes, Redmine is often used as a free, self-hosted, open-source alternative to commercial issue trackers like Jira.

faqs.question: Does Redmine support plugins?
faqs.answer: Yes, Redmine has a large community plugin ecosystem that extends its core functionality significantly.

use_cases.title: Software development issue tracking

use_cases.description: Manage internal IT projects, subprojects, and time tracking across multiple teams.

use_cases.audience: Educational institutions, government agencies, and non-profits

Access to community plugins and themes

enrichment.short_description: Redocly is an API documentation and developer portal platform built around the popular open-source Redoc renderer, helping teams publish, govern, and manage OpenAPI docs.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium, seat-based subscription with separate usage-based add-ons
enrichment.starting_price: Free (Redoc open-source renderer, MIT license); paid plans start at $10/seat/month (Pro)
languages: Platform built primarily in TypeScript/JavaScript; supports OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, GraphQL, and SOAP documentation formats
enrichment.seo_title: Redocly Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and API Documentation Tools
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Explore Redocly's pricing plans, Redoc renderer, developer portal, and API governance tools for teams publishing OpenAPI documentation.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Redocly is an API documentation and developer experience platform founded in 2017, headquartered in Austin, Texas, with roughly 50 employees. The company started as the team behind Redoc, an open-source, MIT-licensed alternative to Swagger UI that renders OpenAPI, and now also AsyncAPI, GraphQL, and SOAP, specifications into clean, three-panel API reference documentation; Redoc remains free to self-host or embed and is widely used, ranking among the most popular API documentation tools on GitHub and npm. On top of the free renderer, Redocly sells a commercial platform for building and governing developer portals, combining several named products: Redoc (the reference documentation renderer), Revel (a developer portal builder supporting Markdown docs, custom React pages, and localization), Reef (an API catalog and governance tool with scorecards and API discovery), and a combined bundle called Realm. Redocly's commercial pricing is seat-based: the Pro plan starts at 10 dollars per seat per month and includes one project, up to 100 pages, custom-domain hosting, mock servers, and linting; the Enterprise plan is 24 dollars per seat per month and adds single sign-on, guest SSO, role-based access control, AI-powered search, analytics, and MCP server support, with page limits raised to 500; an Enterprise+ tier is available with custom, annually billed pricing for organizations that need data residency options, single-tenant hosting, procurement support, security questionnaires, and dedicated professional services. The Revel and Reef add-on products can be purchased individually, roughly 10 dollars per seat per month on Pro or 24 dollars per seat per month on Enterprise, or bundled together as Realm for 18 dollars per seat per month on Pro or 42 dollars per seat per month on Enterprise. Redocly also offers a separate usage-based product for monitoring API reliability, with a free tier covering the first 1,000 requests per month and paid commitment tiers ranging from 100 dollars per month for 20,000 requests up to 1,000 dollars per month for 500,000 requests, plus per-request overage pricing. Redocly's target customers are engineering and API platform teams that need to publish accurate, governed, and well-designed API reference documentation and developer portals at scale, positioning it as an alternative to tools like Swagger UI, Stoplight, and ReadMe.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Founded in 2017 and based in Austin, Texas, Redocly grew out of Redoc, a free, MIT-licensed OpenAPI renderer that remains one of the most widely used API documentation tools on GitHub and npm.

Beyond the free renderer, Redocly's commercial platform combines Redoc, Revel (developer portals), and Reef (API catalog and governance) into modular, seat-based products for engineering and API platform teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The core Redoc renderer turns OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, GraphQL, and SOAP specifications into clean three-panel reference documentation, while Revel adds full developer portal capabilities including Markdown docs and custom React pages.
Reef provides API catalog management and governance scorecards, and Enterprise plans add SSO, RBAC, AI-powered search, and analytics for larger organizations managing many APIs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Redoc itself is free and open source under the MIT license. Redocly's commercial platform starts at 10 dollars per seat per month (Pro) and 24 dollars per seat per month (Enterprise), with a custom-priced Enterprise+ tier for large organizations.

Add-on products Revel and Reef are priced separately per seat, or bundled as Realm; a separate usage-based Respect product for API monitoring starts free for up to 1,000 requests per month and scales to 1,000 dollars per month for 500,000 requests.

features.title: Redoc open-source renderer
features.description: Free, MIT-licensed three-panel API reference documentation generator for OpenAPI and other spec formats.

features.title: Developer portal builder (Revel)
features.description: Combine Markdown documentation, custom React pages, and localization into a full developer portal.

features.title: API catalog and governance (Reef)
features.description: Discover, catalog, and score APIs across an organization for consistency and compliance.

features.title: Multi-format spec support
features.description: Documentation generation for OpenAPI, AsyncAPI, GraphQL, and SOAP specifications.

features.title: Built-in linting and validation
features.description: Automated spec linting helps enforce API design standards before publishing.

features.title: Mock servers
features.description: Generate mock API servers directly from OpenAPI specs for testing and prototyping.

features.title: SSO, RBAC, and AI search
features.description: Enterprise-tier access controls and AI-powered documentation search for larger teams.

features.title: API reliability monitoring (Respect)
features.description: Usage-based monitoring product for tracking API uptime and reliability.

pros: Free, MIT-licensed Redoc renderer is genuinely open source and widely adopted
Clean, professional three-panel documentation output
Modular pricing lets teams pay only for the products they need
Strong OpenAPI governance and linting tooling
Supports multiple spec formats beyond just OpenAPI
Enterprise tier includes SSO and RBAC for larger organizations
cons: Full commercial platform pricing is seat-based and can add up for larger teams
Advanced governance and AI search features are Enterprise-only
Add-on products (Revel, Reef) are priced separately from the core plan
Custom Enterprise+ pricing requires a sales conversation
Usage-based Respect monitoring adds another cost dimension to budget for
Smaller ecosystem and community than more established SaaS competitors like ReadMe

faqs.question: Is Redoc free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Redoc, the underlying renderer, is free and open source under the MIT license and can be self-hosted or embedded.

faqs.question: What does Redocly cost?
faqs.answer: Redocly's commercial platform starts at $10/seat/month (Pro) and $24/seat/month (Enterprise), with custom Enterprise+ pricing available.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Redoc and Redocly?
faqs.answer: Redoc is the free open-source rendering engine; Redocly is the company's full commercial platform for portals, governance, and API management.

faqs.question: Does Redocly support AsyncAPI?
faqs.answer: Yes, in addition to OpenAPI, Redocly supports AsyncAPI, GraphQL, and SOAP documentation.

faqs.question: What is Reef?
faqs.answer: Reef is Redocly's API catalog and governance product, offering discovery and scorecards across an organization's APIs.

faqs.question: What is Revel?
faqs.answer: Revel is Redocly's developer portal builder, supporting Markdown docs, custom React pages, and localization.

faqs.question: Does Redocly offer single sign-on?
faqs.answer: Yes, SSO and guest SSO are included starting at the Enterprise tier.

faqs.question: Who founded Redocly and where is it based?
faqs.answer: Redocly was founded in 2017 and is headquartered in Austin, Texas.

enrichment.short_description: Reflect is a minimalist, networked note-taking app with automatic backlinking, end-to-end encryption, and AI features for capturing and connecting ideas.
enrichment.pricing_model: Flat-rate subscription (single all-inclusive plan) with a free trial
enrichment.seo_title: Reflect Notes Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Backlinking
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Reflect's networked note-taking app works, what it costs, and whether its backlinking and encryption features fit your workflow.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Reflect is a minimalist, networked note-taking application founded in 2021 by Alex MacCaw, previously the founder of Clearbit, which grew to roughly 200 employees and about 50 million dollars in revenue before its acquisition, alongside co-founders known publicly as Ocavue and Vojtech Rinik. The company raised 3 million dollars in seed funding in March 2021 and publicly launched Reflect on April 11, 2022, after roughly a year of development; it remains a small, Austin, Texas-based team of around three to four people. Reflect's core design philosophy centers on networked thought: rather than organizing notes into folders, everything lives in one continuous list, and linking one note to another automatically creates a reciprocal backlink in the referenced note, so notes form a web of connections that mirrors associative human memory rather than a rigid file hierarchy. The app is built for speed, with instant search and quick capture designed to reduce friction between having a thought and recording it, and it includes daily notes, a graph view for visualizing connections, and AI features such as note summarization, writing assistance, and AI-powered chat with your notes as part of the standard plan. Security is a differentiator: Reflect uses end-to-end encryption, meaning notes are encrypted on-device such that even Reflect's own team cannot read user content, which sets it apart from many mainstream note-taking competitors that do not offer true end-to-end encryption. Reflect ships apps for web, macOS, Windows, and iOS, and supports integrations with tools such as Readwise, Google Calendar, and Kindle highlights for pulling external content into notes. Pricing is a single, all-inclusive plan at 10 dollars per month when billed annually, which includes AI features, encryption, mobile apps, and integrations; Reflect does not use a tiered freemium model with feature-gating, though it offers a 14-day free trial so prospective users can test the full product before subscribing. As a small, founder-led company competing against much larger note-taking platforms, Reflect positions itself around privacy, speed, and the backlinking-first networked-notes philosophy popularized by tools like Roam Research and Obsidian.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Reflect was founded in 2021 by Alex MacCaw, previously founder of Clearbit, alongside co-founders Ocavue and Vojtech Rinik, and publicly launched in April 2022 as a fast, networked note-taking app.
The company remains a small, Austin, Texas-based team of roughly three to four people, positioning Reflect as a founder-led, privacy-focused alternative to larger note-taking platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Reflect's defining feature is automatic backlinking: linking one note to another creates a reciprocal link in both notes, forming a network of connected ideas rather than a folder hierarchy.

The app includes end-to-end encryption, daily notes, a visual graph view, AI-powered summarization and chat, and integrations with Readwise, Google Calendar, and Kindle highlights, all included in a single plan.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Reflect uses a single, flat-rate pricing model at 10 dollars per month when billed annually, which includes every feature: AI tools, end-to-end encryption, mobile apps, and integrations.
There is no permanent free tier, but Reflect offers a 14-day free trial with full feature access before requiring a subscription.

features.title: Automatic backlinking
features.description: Linking to another note automatically creates a reciprocal backlink, forming a connected network of ideas.

features.title: End-to-end encryption
features.description: Notes are encrypted on-device so that even Reflect's team cannot access user content.

features.title: AI-powered summarization and chat
features.description: Built-in AI tools help summarize notes and let users query their notes conversationally.

features.title: Daily notes and graph view
features.description: Daily journaling entries and a visual graph help users track and explore their thinking over time.

features.title: Cross-platform apps
features.description: Native apps are available for web, macOS, Windows, and iOS.

features.title: Fast quick capture and search
features.description: The app is optimized for minimal friction between having a thought and recording it.

features.title: Third-party integrations
features.description: Connects with Readwise, Google Calendar, and Kindle highlights to pull external content into notes.

pros: True end-to-end encryption, unusual among mainstream note-taking apps
Automatic backlinking creates a genuinely networked note structure
Fast, minimalist interface focused on quick capture
Single flat-rate plan with no feature gating
AI features included at no extra cost
Built by an experienced founder (Clearbit) with a small, focused team
cons: No permanently free tier, only a 14-day trial
Small team size may mean slower feature development and support response
Lacks some advanced organizational features found in tools like Notion
Mobile app support is currently limited to iOS, with no native Android app
Steeper conceptual learning curve for users unfamiliar with networked note-taking
Smaller integration ecosystem than larger competitors

faqs.question: Is Reflect free?
faqs.answer: Reflect has no permanent free tier but offers a 14-day free trial; after that it costs $10/month billed annually.

faqs.question: Who created Reflect?
faqs.answer: Reflect was founded in 2021 by Alex MacCaw, previously founder of Clearbit, along with co-founders Ocavue and Vojtech Rinik.

faqs.question: Does Reflect encrypt notes?
faqs.answer: Yes, Reflect uses end-to-end encryption so notes are only readable by the user, not by Reflect itself.

faqs.question: Does Reflect have an Android app?
faqs.answer: Reflect currently offers an iOS app but no native Android app.

faqs.question: What is backlinking in Reflect?
faqs.answer: Backlinking automatically creates a reciprocal link in a referenced note whenever you link to it from another note.

faqs.question: Does Reflect include AI features?
faqs.answer: Yes, AI summarization and chat features are included in Reflect's single subscription plan.

faqs.question: Can I import Readwise highlights into Reflect?
faqs.answer: Yes, Reflect integrates with Readwise as well as Google Calendar and Kindle highlights.

faqs.question: Where is Reflect based?
faqs.answer: Reflect is based in Austin, Texas, with a small team of about three to four people.

enrichment.short_description: Regie.ai is an AI-powered sales engagement platform that automates prospecting, content personalization, and multi-channel outreach for B2B sales teams.
enrichment.pricing_model: Per-seat subscription with minimum seat counts, plus custom enterprise pricing
enrichment.starting_price: From $180/user/month (10-seat minimum, approximately $21,600/year) for the AI SEP plan
enrichment.seo_title: Regie.ai Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and AI Sales Tools
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn what Regie.ai costs, how its AI sales agents and RegieOne platform work, and whether it fits your B2B sales prospecting stack.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Regie.ai is an AI-powered sales engagement and prospecting platform founded in 2020 by Dr. Srinath Sridhar, an AI scientist, and Matt Millen, a veteran enterprise sales leader; the company is headquartered in San Francisco, California, and employs roughly 100 people across multiple continents as of late 2025. Regie.ai has raised a total of about 65.6 million dollars in venture funding, including a 30 million dollar Series B round announced in February 2025 co-led by Scale Venture Partners and Foundation Capital, with additional backing from Khosla Ventures, StepStone Group, TriplePoint Capital, South Park Commons, and Day One Ventures; the company reported roughly 300 percent year-over-year ARR growth around the time of that Series B raise. The platform's core value proposition is combining a contact database of more than 220 million B2B records with AI agents that research prospects, draft personalized outbound emails, sequence multi-touch follow-ups, and manage outreach cadences at scale, aiming to replace or augment manual SDR (sales development representative) prospecting work. In 2025 Regie.ai introduced RegieOne, a unified platform positioning for smarter prospecting that ties together its AI agents, contact data, and content generation capabilities into a single workflow rather than separate point tools. Pricing is structured in tiers built around seats and use case: the entry-level AI SEP (Sales Engagement Platform) plan starts at 180 dollars per user per month with a 10-seat minimum, working out to roughly 21,600 dollars per year before add-ons; a more advanced Force Multiplier Rep tier is priced at 499 dollars per user per month for teams that want deeper AI agent automation; and RegieOne enterprise pricing is fully custom and quote-based for larger sales organizations. Because pricing is seat-based with meaningful minimums, Regie.ai is generally positioned for mid-market and enterprise B2B sales teams with dedicated SDR or outbound functions rather than solo users or very small sales teams. Competitors in the AI sales engagement and AI SDR space include tools like Outreach, Salesloft, and Apollo, along with various emerging AI SDR agent startups, and Regie.ai differentiates primarily through its combination of proprietary contact data and generative AI content and outreach automation built specifically for revenue teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Regie.ai was founded in 2020 by Dr. Srinath Sridhar and Matt Millen and is headquartered in San Francisco, employing roughly 100 people as of late 2025. The company has raised about 65.6 million dollars in total funding, including a 30 million dollar Series B in February 2025.
The platform combines a 220 million-plus contact database with AI agents for prospecting, content generation, and outreach sequencing, unified in 2025 under a platform called RegieOne.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Regie.ai's AI agents research prospects and draft personalized outbound email content, then sequence multi-channel follow-ups automatically, reducing manual SDR workload.
RegieOne ties together the contact database, AI content generation, and outreach automation into one workflow, with reporting on pipeline and ARR impact for sales leaders.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Regie.ai's entry-level AI SEP plan starts at 180 dollars per user per month with a 10-seat minimum, roughly 21,600 dollars per year before add-ons.
The Force Multiplier Rep tier costs 499 dollars per user per month for deeper AI agent automation, while full RegieOne enterprise deployments are custom-priced.

enrichment.short_description: RELATE is an open-source, Django-based online courseware platform created by Andreas Kloeckner for building and delivering interactive university-style courses.
languages: Built with Python/Django; course content authored in YAML and Markdown
enrichment.seo_title: RELATE Courseware Review: Open-Source Online Course Platform
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn about RELATE, Andreas Kloeckner's open-source Django courseware platform for interactive university course delivery, grading, and content.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: RELATE is a free, open-source, web-based courseware platform created by Andreas Kloeckner, a computer science professor, primarily for delivering interactive university-level online courses; the project's documentation carries a copyright notice spanning 2014 through 2025, indicating over a decade of active development, and its source code is maintained publicly on GitHub. Built on the Python-based Django web framework, RELATE is a niche, academic tool rather than a mainstream commercial SaaS product, and it is distributed at no cost for institutions and instructors willing to self-host it on their own infrastructure. The platform's central concept is the flow, a structured sequence of pages that combines static instructional content such as text and videos with interactive elements such as quizzes and sandboxed, auto-graded coding exercises, allowing instructors to build self-paced or scheduled learning modules. Course content itself is authored in a simple, version-controllable, text-based format using YAML for structure and Markdown for content, which lets instructors track changes to their courses using Git; RELATE explicitly supports Git-based workflows so instructors can preview and edit new course content while enrolled students continue to see a stable prior version until changes are published. Additional features include a built-in grade book, a class calendar, live in-class quiz functionality, sandboxed code execution with automatic grading and plotting support for programming assignments, human-in-the-loop grading workflows for open-ended responses, and support for hosting multiple independent courses on a single RELATE installation. The platform also includes in-class messaging via XMPP for real-time classroom interaction. Because RELATE is a specialized academic project rather than a general-purpose commercial product, it lacks the marketing presence, company backing, pricing tiers, or customer support infrastructure of mainstream learning management systems such as Canvas, Moodle, or Blackboard, and it is best understood as a purpose-built tool for technical and STEM courses, particularly ones with programming or computational components, taught by instructors comfortable with self-hosting Django applications and authoring course content in text-based formats. Its most notable adopter has been Kloeckner's own courses at the University of Illinois Urbana-Champaign, where RELATE has been used for numerical methods and scientific computing instruction.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RELATE is an open-source Django courseware platform created by Andreas Kloeckner, with development documented from 2014 through 2025, primarily used for delivering technical and STEM university courses online.

Unlike mainstream commercial learning management systems, RELATE is a niche academic tool with no company, sales team, or pricing structure behind it; it is distributed freely on GitHub for self-hosting.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: RELATE organizes courses into flows, sequences of static content and interactive elements such as quizzes and sandboxed, auto-graded coding exercises, well suited to programming-heavy coursework.

Course content is authored in YAML and Markdown and versioned with Git, and the platform includes a grade book, class calendar, live in-class quizzes, and support for hosting multiple courses on one installation.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: RELATE is entirely free and open source, with no licensing fees, tiers, or commercial vendor.
Any cost comes from self-hosting infrastructure and the technical effort required to deploy and maintain a Django application.

Less polished user interface than mainstream commercial LMS platforms
Smaller community and documentation base than Moodle or Canvas
Not designed for non-technical or K-12 educational contexts

faqs.answer: Yes, RELATE is free and open source, distributed via GitHub for self-hosting.

faqs.answer: RELATE was created by Andreas Kloeckner, a computer science professor.

enrichment.short_description: RelativityOne is a cloud-based, AI-powered e-discovery and legal data intelligence platform from Relativity, used by law firms, corporations, and government agencies.
enrichment.pricing_model: Custom enterprise pricing, pay-as-you-go or 1-3 year commitments
enrichment.seo_title: RelativityOne Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and E-Discovery Tools
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Learn how RelativityOne's cloud e-discovery platform works, who uses it, and how its custom pricing compares for legal and compliance teams.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: RelativityOne is the cloud-based, software-as-a-service version of Relativity, an e-discovery and legal data intelligence platform originally built by kCura, a Chicago-based software consulting firm founded in 2001 by Andrew Sieja. The company rebranded from kCura to Relativity in August 2017, taking the name of its flagship product, and remains headquartered in Chicago, Illinois, with roughly 1,800 to 1,900 employees as of 2025-2026 spread across the United States, Poland, the United Kingdom, and other locations; independent estimates put the company's annual recurring revenue near 235.9 million dollars with a valuation reported around 3.6 billion dollars. RelativityOne is hosted on Microsoft Azure and offers the full functionality of the original on-premises Relativity Server product as a secure, cloud-native SaaS platform, adding elastic scalability, built-in AI-powered review and analytics capabilities, and reduced infrastructure management burden for customers; Relativity also continues to offer Relativity Server as an on-premises deployment option for organizations with specific data residency or infrastructure requirements. The platform is used to manage the discovery, review, and analysis of large volumes of electronic data in litigation, internal investigations, and regulatory compliance matters, combining document review workflows, AI-assisted categorization and prioritization, data visualization, and case analytics. Relativity reports that RelativityOne and its broader platform serve more than 180,000 users across more than 40 countries, including customers such as the U.S. Department of Justice, more than 70 Fortune 100 companies, and 198 of the Am Law 200 largest law firms, making it one of the dominant platforms in the legal e-discovery software category alongside competitors like Everlaw, Reveal (Logikcull), and DISCO. Pricing for RelativityOne is not publicly published; Relativity structures it around flexible, predictable enterprise pricing models, offering customers the choice of pay-as-you-go consumption pricing or discounted one-year or three-year committed contracts, with exact costs determined by data volume, user counts, and workspace configuration through direct sales engagement. Because of its scale, AI capabilities, and deep adoption among major law firms and corporate legal departments, RelativityOne is generally positioned as an enterprise-grade platform rather than a self-serve tool for small legal teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: RelativityOne traces back to kCura, a Chicago software firm founded in 2001 by Andrew Sieja, which rebranded as Relativity in 2017 after its flagship e-discovery product. The company now has roughly 1,800-1,900 employees and remains headquartered in Chicago.

RelativityOne is the cloud-native, Azure-hosted version of the company's e-discovery platform, serving more than 180,000 users across 40-plus countries, including major law firms, corporations, and government agencies.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform combines document review workflows, AI-assisted categorization and prioritization, data visualization, and case analytics to help legal teams manage large volumes of electronic data in litigation, investigations, and compliance matters.
RelativityOne is hosted on Microsoft Azure for elastic scalability, while Relativity Server remains available as an on-premises option for organizations with specific data residency needs.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: RelativityOne pricing is not publicly disclosed and is structured around enterprise sales engagements, with customers choosing between pay-as-you-go consumption pricing or discounted 1-year or 3-year commitments.
Final cost depends on factors such as data volume, number of users, and workspace configuration, determined through direct consultation with Relativity's sales team.

enrichment.short_description: Relay is a business banking and money-management platform offering fee-free checking, multiple sub-accounts, cards, and cash-flow tools for small businesses.
enrichment.pricing_model: Freemium subscription: free Starter tier plus paid Grow and Scale plans
enrichment.seo_title: Relay Financial Review 2026: Pricing, Features, and Business Banking
enrichment.seo_meta_description: See how Relay's business banking platform works, what its Starter, Grow, and Scale plans cost, and which features fit growing businesses.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Relay, also known as Relay Financial, is a business banking and cash-flow management platform founded in 2018 and headquartered in Toronto, Ontario, Canada. The company built an all-in-one banking product aimed at small and mid-sized businesses that need more control over multi-account cash management than a typical single-account business checking product provides; as with most U.S.-facing fintech banking products, Relay's accounts are held and insured through partner banks rather than Relay itself holding a banking charter. The platform's core feature is support for up to 20 individual checking accounts on the free plan, rising to up to 50 on higher tiers, so businesses can separate funds for taxes, payroll, reserves, and specific projects without opening accounts at multiple banks, alongside up to 50 virtual or physical Visa debit cards for employee spend control. Relay supports depositing funds via ACH transfer, wire, check, cash, and payouts from payment processors such as PayPal, Stripe, and Square, and supports sending money via ACH, wire, and check; it also syncs transaction data directly into QuickBooks Online and Xero for bookkeeping and reconciliation. Relay's pricing follows a three-tier freemium model: the Starter plan is free and includes up to 20 checking accounts and basic ACH functionality with no monthly account fees, no overdraft fees, and no minimum balance requirement; the Grow plan is 30 dollars per month and adds free outgoing domestic wires, automated bill pay, same-day ACH, and a higher-yield savings account around 1.55 percent APY; and the Scale plan is 90 dollars per month, which raises the checking account limit to 50, increases savings APY further to around 2.68 percent, and unlocks more advanced automation and financial workflow tools. Relay also offers up to two savings accounts with yields reported in the roughly 1 to 3 percent APY range depending on plan tier. Because it focuses specifically on multi-account organization, integrated bill pay, and accounting-software syncing rather than lending or investment products, Relay is generally positioned as a cash-flow and treasury-management-oriented alternative to traditional business checking accounts and other fintech competitors such as Mercury, Novo, and Bluevine.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Relay was founded in 2018 and is headquartered in Toronto, Ontario, Canada, offering an all-in-one business banking platform focused on multi-account cash-flow organization for small and mid-sized businesses.

Rather than competing on lending or investment products, Relay differentiates through account structure flexibility, integrated bill pay, and direct syncing with accounting software like QuickBooks Online and Xero.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Relay lets businesses open up to 20 (Starter) or 50 (Scale) individual checking accounts to separate funds for taxes, payroll, and specific projects, along with up to 50 virtual or physical Visa debit cards for team spend control.

The platform supports ACH, wire, and check transfers in both directions, deposits from payment processors like Stripe, PayPal, and Square, and direct syncing with QuickBooks Online and Xero for bookkeeping.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Relay's Starter plan is free with no monthly fees, overdraft fees, or minimum balance, supporting up to 20 checking accounts and basic ACH.

The Grow plan costs $30/month and adds free domestic wires, automated bill pay, and same-day ACH, while the Scale plan at $90/month raises account limits to 50 and unlocks advanced automation and higher savings APY.

faqs.answer: Yes, Relay's Starter plan is free with no monthly fees, overdraft fees, or minimum balance.
